title: Special cycles on simple Shimura varieties
speaker: Wei Zhang
abstract: Abstract: This is a work in progress inspired by the the arithmetic Gan--Gross--Prasad conjecture where one is interested in the arithmetic diagonal cycle on the product of two Shimura varieties. We study special cycles on simple Shimura varieties attached to central simple algebras with an involution of second kind. We study some local questions arising from the relative trace formula approach.

proof_stages.label: Bloch-Kato/Selmer motivation and the Heegner-point triangle
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the conjectural equality between Selmer-group dimension and the order of vanishing of an L-function, then recalls the elliptic-curve Heegner-point case where formulas for L-values and Euler-system or congruence arguments connect special cycles to Selmer groups.

proof_stages.label: Choice of a higher-dimensional generalization
proof_stages.summary: After contrasting with the Gan-Gross-Prasad tensor-product setting and noting the role of p-adic L-functions for Selmer nontriviality, the speaker proposes a more direct higher-dimensional analogue of the Gross-Zagier torus construction by replacing GL_n with suitable unitary outer forms over number fields.

proof_stages.label: Simple Shimura varieties from algebras with involution
proof_stages.summary: The required Shimura varieties are built from a central simple algebra B over a CM quadratic extension F'/F with an involution of the second kind, a skew-Hermitian element defining a unitary similitude group G, and signature conditions that give PEL-type simple Shimura varieties with useful compactness and uniformization features.

proof_stages.label: Special cycles via a second quadratic extension
proof_stages.summary: Introducing a second totally real quadratic extension E/F and the biquadratic algebra E' = EF', the centralizer B' of E' in B defines a subgroup H inside G and hence an embedding of Shimura data whose image is the special cycle to be studied.

proof_stages.label: Relative trace formula and local reduction
proof_stages.summary: The expected global connection with L-functions is to be made by a relative trace formula, but the talk turns to the local unramified theory, where the relevant orbital integrals and local Rapoport-Zink-type moduli problems can be stated explicitly.

proof_stages.label: Linear local arithmetic fundamental lemma
proof_stages.summary: In the split/inert local case F' = F × F and E/F unramified inert, the speaker defines orbital integrals for GL_n(F) with H = GL_{n/2}(E), matches them to orbital integrals on GL_n(E) with a quadratic character, states a fundamental-lemma identity for unit functions, and formulates an arithmetic fundamental lemma equating the derivative orbital integral with an intersection number of local cycles.

proof_stages.label: Unitary local case and comparison with known local-density conjectures
proof_stages.summary: The parallel unitary case replaces the linear groups by U_n and U_{n/2} × U_{n/2}, requires stabilization because of nontrivial stabilizer cohomology, admits a fundamental-lemma statement, and has an arithmetic version shown to be equivalent to an existing conjecture on special-divisor intersections/local densities.

examples.name: Tensor powers of H^1 of a curve
examples.note: The speaker uses tensor powers of the first cohomology of a higher-genus curve, after a large Tate twist, to illustrate how the Bloch-Kato prediction would imply strong vanishing statements relevant to nonabelian Chabauty.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes the general weight/absolute-convergence argument to tensor powers of H^1 of a curve, checks that a sufficiently large twist has nonnegative weight, and derives the expected Selmer triviality and Chabauty/Faltings consequence.

passes.reason: Tensor powers of H^1 of a curve are a standard motivic/Galois-representation construction used as motivation, not as a failure case.
ordinary tensor-power cohomology motives of a curve are used as motivation for expected Selmer vanishing, not as an obstruction or special classification case.

examples.name: Classical elliptic-curve Heegner-point/Gross-Zagier model
examples.note: The elliptic curve over Q with an imaginary quadratic field supplies the Heegner-point triangle—cycles, L-values, and Selmer groups—from which the higher-dimensional construction is explicitly modeled.
examples.engagement_reason: He lays out the elliptic-curve over Q with imaginary quadratic K case, separates rank zero and rank one, and traces how Heegner points/Gross-Zagier-type formulas and Euler-system or congruence arguments connect L-values to Selmer groups.

passes.reason: The elliptic-curve Heegner-point/Gross-Zagier setting is the low-dimensional base model for the higher-dimensional generalization, not a counterexample.
the elliptic-curve Heegner-point/Gross-Zagier setup is a standard positive prototype for the desired triangle of ideas, not a sporadic or adversarial example.

Heegner points are CM special points with maximal torus/endomorphism symmetry, used here as the positive Gross-Zagier-Kolyvagin model.
The GL2/elliptic-curve Heegner-point case is the lowest-dimensional base model for the higher-dimensional analogy.
It is used as the classical prototype, and the elliptic-curve Heegner-point setting is the lowest-dimensional base case for the later higher-dimensional generalization.

examples.name: Unitary Gan-Gross-Prasad tensor-product situation
examples.note: The Gan-Gross-Prasad Rankin-Selberg representation for GL_{n-1} times GL_n is presented as a known higher-dimensional direction, mainly to contrast with the alternative generalization pursued in the talk.
examples.engagement_reason: He describes the unitary Gan-Gross-Prasad setup as a tensor product of n−1 and n dimensional Galois representations with Rankin-Selberg L-function, but does not carry out a calculation or construction on it.

passes.reason: The unitary Gan-Gross-Prasad tensor-product case is a systematic higher-dimensional Rankin-Selberg setting used as a comparison point.

examples.name: Function-field direct GL_n replacement
examples.note: The direct replacement of the torus-in-GL_2 picture by Res GL_{n/2} inside GL_n works over function fields, and its failure to produce Shimura varieties over number fields visibly motivates passing to unitary outer forms.
examples.engagement_reason: He concretely replaces the Gross-Zagier torus-in-GL2 picture by a Res GL_{n/2} inside GL_n construction, explains the embedding via a basis, and notes why it works in function fields but not as a number-field Shimura-variety construction.

passes.reason: The direct GL_n replacement over function fields is presented as a working systematic analogue rather than an obstruction or sporadic case.
the function-field direct GL_n replacement is presented as a regular analogue that works in function fields, not as a counterexample.
The direct GL_n replacement is cited as a positive function-field analogue, despite not giving a number-field Shimura-variety construction.

examples.name: Matrix algebra with transpose-conjugate involution
examples.note: The special case B = M_n(F') with conjugate-transpose involution makes the central-simple-algebra Shimura datum concrete as the familiar unitary group attached to a Hermitian form.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses B=M_n(F') with transpose-conjugate involution as an explicit example, then twists by a skew-Hermitian form and identifies the resulting similitude/unitary group.

passes.reason: The matrix algebra with transpose-conjugate involution is the split/Brauer-trivial central-simple-algebra case giving the usual unitary group.
the matrix algebra with transpose-conjugate involution is the split, trivial-Brauer-class model among central simple algebras with involution.

examples.name: Division algebra giving Drinfeld upper-half-space uniformization
examples.note: A division algebra with Hasse invariant 1/n at a split place illustrates the extra flexibility of the simple-algebra construction and why it can lead to compact Shimura varieties with Drinfeld uniformization.
examples.engagement_reason: He states that taking a division algebra with Hasse invariant 1/n gives Drinfeld upper-half-space uniformization and a compact Shimura variety, but does not work through that example.

passes.reason: The local division algebra of invariant 1/n is the maximally non-split degree-n case enabling Drinfeld upper-half-space uniformization.
a degree-n division algebra with Hasse invariant 1/n is the maximally non-split inner form enabling Drinfeld upper-half-space uniformization.

examples.name: n = 2 biquadratic specialization
examples.note: When n = 2 and F = Q, the groups reduce up to central factors to an anisotropic torus inside a quaternion algebra, showing concretely how the new special cycles recover the Gross-Zagier-style torus-in-GL_2 picture.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes to n=2, introduces the third quadratic field in the biquadratic extension, writes explicit formulas for G and H, and identifies the pair with the Gross-Zagier-type torus inside a quaternionic inner form of GL2.

passes.reason: The n=2 biquadratic specialization is the minimal-dimensional case reducing essentially to the classical anisotropic-torus-inside-quaternionic-GL2 Gross-Zagier model.
the n=2 biquadratic case is the minimal even case and collapses to the torus-in-quaternion Gross-Zagier-type pair.
The n=2 specialization is the minimal even-n case, where the construction collapses to a torus/Gross-Zagier-type situation.
The n=2 biquadratic case is the minimal even-rank boundary/base case reducing to the Gross-Zagier torus-in-quaternion pair.
